title: Types & Variables
subtitle: From static typing to dynamic — but not lawless

sections.java: // Declared type, fixed forever int count = 42; String name = "Ada"; final double RATE = 0.05; // Reassigning to wrong type = compile error count = "oops"; // ✗ won't compile
sections.python: # No type keyword — inferred at runtime count = 42 name = "Ada" RATE = 0.05 # UPPER = convention for const # Names are just labels — rebind freely count = "oops" # ✓ legal, count is now a str

sections.title: Variables Are Labels, Not Boxes
sections.content: In Java, a variable is a typed slot in memory. In Python, a variable is just a name bound to an object. x = 5 doesn't put 5 into a box called x — it points the name x at an int object. This is why a = b for a list makes both names point at the same list. See Module 03 for the aliasing gotcha.

sections.java: // Primitives vs boxed objects int x = 5; // primitive Integer y = 5; // boxed object long big = 9_000_000_000L; // int overflows silently at 2^31 int overflow = Integer.MAX_VALUE + 1;
sections.python: # Everything is an object — no primitives x = 5 # int (full object) y = 5 # same — no boxing big = 9_000_000_000 # no L suffix # ints are arbitrary precision — never overflow huge = 2 ** 200 # just works

sections.title: Type Hints: Documentation That Tools Check
sections.content: Python type hints (x: int) are not enforced at runtime — the interpreter ignores them. They exist for your IDE, for mypy/pyright static checkers, and for human readers. Think of them like Java generics that the JVM erases — present in source, gone at execution. Per this course's rules, use them everywhere.

sections.java: // == compares references for objects String a = new String("hi"); String b = new String("hi"); a == b; // false (different refs) a.equals(b); // true (value equal)
sections.python: # == compares VALUES, is compares identity a = "hi" b = "hi" a == b # True (value equal) a is b # identity — like Java ==

sections.title: == and is Are Swapped From Your Instinct
sections.content: In Java, == is identity and .equals() is value. In Python it's flipped: == is value equality (calls __eq__, like .equals()) and is is identity (same object in memory). Use is only for singletons: if x is None, never if x == None.

sections.question: What does `0.1 + 0.2 == 0.3` evaluate to in Python?
options: False — floats are IEEE-754, same as Java's double
True only with type hints enabled
sections.explanation: Python's float is a 64-bit IEEE-754 double — exactly like Java's double. So 0.1 + 0.2 is 0.30000000000000004 and the comparison is False. For exact money math, use decimal.Decimal just as you'd reach for BigDecimal in Java.
